decisive_weakness: The disclosure strings differ in length — the AI condition announces 'Disclosure: this article was generated by an AI writing assistant and reviewed by a human editor' while the human condition announces only 'Written by a staff journalist' — producing an announcement-duration confound that makes the primary behavioral measure (dwell-onset latency) uninterpretable as evidence of semantic content effects.

evidence_span.quote: The human-framed condition ('Written by a staff journalist') is substantially shorter than the AI disclosure string, which introduces a confound the rehearsal cannot ignore: announcement duration differs across conditions by several seconds. A participant in the AI condition would plausibly experience a longer interruption before resuming heading navigation, and any condition difference in dwell-onset latency could partly reflect announcement length rather than content.

criticisms.violated_criterion: confound architecture — a variable that explains the results equally well is not controlled
criticisms.why_matters: The primary quantitative behavioral measure is 'time from page-load completion to first body-text dwell,' compared across conditions via Friedman test. Because the AI disclosure string is approximately three times longer than the human-framed string, any observed latency difference is causally ambiguous: it could reflect semantic processing of AI authorship, or simply the mechanical fact that a longer audio stream delays the moment the participant can resume heading navigation. No post-hoc analysis can disentangle these after data collection because announcement duration and semantic content are perfectly collinear within the design as written.
criticisms.required_change: Equalize announcement duration across all three conditions by padding the human-framed string to the same word count (e.g., 'Written by a staff journalist who researched and fact-checked this article') or by measuring and covarying actual announcement duration in seconds as logged from the Zoom audio, with a supplementary analysis excluding trials where duration differed by more than one second from the condition mean.

evidence_span.quote: For the late-banner condition specifically, the wizard must detect in real time that the participant's cursor has passed the second H2, and then fire the injection — but a participant who jumps rapidly from H2 to H2 using the H key could plausibly traverse all three headings in under 4 seconds, giving the wizard almost no window to fire between the second and third heading. The late-banner timing precision would reasonably degrade under fast navigators, potentially collapsing the early/late distinction for a subset of trials.

criticisms.violated_criterion: manipulation integrity — the independent variable cannot be delivered reliably across participants
criticisms.why_matters: The research question explicitly turns on whether banner placement relative to the heading tree ('where the disclosure sits in the heading tree') modulates navigation strategy. If the late-banner condition collapses into a randomly-timed or post-navigation injection for fast-navigating participants, the early-versus-late contrast — which is the mechanistic claim of the study — cannot be evaluated. A within-subject Friedman test on three conditions where one condition is operationally unstable will produce uninterpretable pairwise contrasts between early-AI and late-AI, which is the comparison that distinguishes this study from prior disclosure-timing literature.
criticisms.required_change: Replace human-judgment trigger timing for the late condition with an automated cursor-position event: instrument the prototype HTML to emit a server-sent event when the screen reader's focus element matches the second H2's ID, and have the server auto-inject the late banner on that event. This removes wizard reaction-time variance entirely. Alternatively, define 'late' as a fixed elapsed-time delay (e.g., 15 seconds post-load) and pre-specify that trials where the participant reached the third H2 before the injection are excluded from the late-condition analysis with this exclusion criterion registered before data collection.

evidence_span.quote: by the third trial, a participant would reasonably have inferred that authorship framing is the experimental variable of interest, and their credibility ratings in trial 3 could plausibly reflect hypothesis-guessing rather than authentic judgment. The Latin-square counterbalancing addresses order effects at the group level but does not neutralize this within-participant awareness drift.

criticisms.violated_criterion: demand characteristics — within-subject design with semantically transparent conditions produces hypothesis-confirming response bias
criticisms.why_matters: Each participant receives all three conditions, and the conditions differ only in announced authorship. By trial 2 or 3, a participant has already heard one or two disclosure variants and will reasonably infer that authorship is the variable being tested. The 7-item credibility scale then measures a mix of authentic credibility judgment and strategic responding. The debrief question 'whether the participant noticed any difference between articles' will retrospectively reveal how many participants guessed the manipulation — but by then the credibility data are already collected and cannot be corrected. With n=8 and all participants contributing trial-3 data, demand-characteristic contamination in the final condition of each participant's sequence is not a minor noise source; it is structurally guaranteed for at least the subset of participants who notice the pattern.
criticisms.required_change: Add a post-experiment suspicion probe administered before the debrief explanation ('Did you form any impression of what the study was examining?') and pre-register an exclusion criterion: participants who correctly name authorship disclosure as the experimental variable before debriefing are excluded from credibility rating analysis. If anticipated exclusions would reduce analyzable n below 6, switch to a between-subjects design for the credibility measure while retaining within-subject counterbalancing only for the behavioral navigation measures.

evidence_span.quote: Compare these measures across the three conditions (early-AI, late-AI, human) using a within-subject Friedman test across the 8 participants, with individual condition contrasts via Wilcoxon signed-rank.

criticisms.violated_criterion: power analysis absent where effect claims are quantitative
criticisms.why_matters: The analysis plan specifies inferential tests (Friedman, Wilcoxon signed-rank) applied to n=8 with three conditions, producing 8 trial-triplets. No power analysis or effect-size justification appears anywhere in the prototype spec, branch card, or audit. With n=8, the Friedman test has very low power to detect anything below a large effect (f > 0.4 by conventional benchmarks). The one-sentence claim asserts 'measurable ways' of shifting navigation strategies and trust ratings — a quantitative claim — but the design as specified cannot reliably detect small or medium effects. A null result will be uninterpretable: it cannot distinguish 'disclosure has no effect' from 'this design was underpowered to detect a plausible effect.'
criticisms.required_change: Either (a) conduct an a priori power analysis citing an effect-size estimate from the closest available prior work on disclosure and credibility judgment (e.g., Liang et al. 2024 cited in the branch card), determine the required n, and recruit accordingly, or (b) reframe the contribution explicitly as a qualitative feasibility study with no inferential claims, remove the Friedman and Wilcoxon tests from the analysis plan, and replace them with descriptive tabulation plus qualitative thematic analysis of think-aloud data. The current hybrid — small n with inferential statistics — satisfies neither standard.

evidence_span.quote: Two coders independently apply a 4-category scheme to each clip: (A) no verbal acknowledgment of disclosure, (B) verbal acknowledgment without evaluative language (e.g., 'oh, it said something'), (C) verbal acknowledgment with positive or neutral evaluation ('okay, AI-assisted, fine'), (D) verbal acknowledgment with skeptical or adversarial language ('AI? Let me check the sources'). Compute inter-rater agreement (Cohen's kappa) on the 24 clips.

criticisms.violated_criterion: analysis plan placeholder — codebook reliability plan is post-hoc and thresholds for acceptable agreement are unspecified
criticisms.why_matters: The 4-category scheme distinguishes B from C on the basis of 'evaluative language' and C from D on the basis of 'skeptical or adversarial language,' but no codebook defining these category boundaries is described in the materials list. The two coders will begin coding without calibration examples or anchor utterances, and Cohen's kappa is computed after coding is complete with no pre-specified minimum acceptable kappa and no protocol for adjudicating disagreements. If kappa falls below 0.60, the think-aloud signal — which is the primary evidence for the adversarial-check relational mode predicted by the branch hypothesis — is unreliable, but the proposal contains no stopping rule or fallback for this scenario.
criticisms.required_change: Before data collection, produce a written codebook with at least three anchor examples per category drawn from pilot sessions or analogous transcripts, conduct a calibration pass where both coders apply the scheme to 5 practice clips and discuss disagreements until reaching consensus, and pre-register a minimum acceptable kappa of 0.70 with a specified adjudication procedure (e.g., third coder as tiebreaker) for clips that remain in disagreement after one reconciliation round.
